title.en: A more junior person changed your mind
title.es: Una persona más junior cambió tu opinión

question.en: Describe a time a more junior person changed your mind on something you were confident about. How did you realize they were right?
question.es: Describe una ocasión en que una persona más junior cambió tu opinión sobre algo en lo que tenías confianza. ¿Cómo te diste cuenta de que tenían razón?

must_include: the specific position the candidate held before the conversation — what they believed and why they were confident in it
the specific argument or evidence the junior person offered — not 'they had a good point' but what exactly they said or showed
the moment of realization — what specifically shifted the candidate's view, and whether that happened in the conversation or after reflection
what the candidate said to the junior person to acknowledge they had changed their position — not just an internal shift, but an external signal
whether the candidate gave the junior person credit for the change in any public or visible way — e.g., in a team meeting, in a document, in a message to their manager
must_avoid: framing the junior person's contribution as minor or as something the candidate would have found eventually ('they just surfaced it a bit earlier than I would have')
telling the story entirely from the candidate's internal perspective — the junior person should appear as a full actor with a real contribution, not as a catalyst for the candidate's own growth
vague attribution such as 'the team helped me see it differently' when the story is about one specific junior person
omitting what the candidate did with the credit — listening and changing internally without public acknowledgment is incomplete RFI
